name: Quebec City
summary: Quebec City is a French-speaking storybook town on a cliff above the St. Lawrence River, with the only city walls left standing in North America north of Mexico. You can wander cobblestone streets, gaze up at the fairy-tale Chateau Frontenac, and stand near a waterfall taller than Niagara. It was founded way back in 1608, making it one of the oldest cities in Canada.
funFacts: Quebec City still has its full stone walls, gates, and cannons. It is the only walled city left in North America north of Mexico.
The Chateau Frontenac holds the Guinness record as the most photographed hotel on Earth, with copper roofs and pointy turrets like a real castle.
Montmorency Falls drops 83 metres, about 30 metres taller than Niagara Falls, and you can ride a cable car or zoom across on a zipline.
Quebec City was founded in 1608 by French explorer Samuel de Champlain, making it more than 400 years old and the heart of New France.
Every winter a giant snowman named Bonhomme Carnaval gets handed the keys to the city to start a huge ice and snow festival.
thingsToDo: Walk along the old stone ramparts and through the city gates of Old Quebec, spotting cannons and bastions left over from 1700s battles.
Ride the funicular, the oldest cable railway in North America, between Lower Town and Upper Town to reach the Chateau Frontenac.
Explore Place Royale in Lower Town, where Champlain set up his trading post in 1608, and find the giant painted mural of Quebec history.
Roam the Plains of Abraham battlefield park with picnic spots, century-old maples, and a museum full of interactive games and treasure hunts.
Take a cable car up Montmorency Falls and cross the suspension footbridge right over the roaring water at the very top.
Drive a loop around Ile d'Orleans for strawberry farms, the famous chocolaterie, and pretty riverside villages just 15 minutes from town.

localWord.meaning: Hello (the friendly French greeting you will hear everywhere in Quebec)

images.alt: The grand green-roofed Château Frontenac hotel towers over the rooftops of Quebec City beside the wide St. Lawrence River.
Old stone houses with steep roofs glow under warm lights around Place Royale square at night in Old Quebec.
A narrow cobbled lane in the Petit-Champlain quarter is lined with colourful little shops and flower boxes.
The star-shaped stone fortress walls of the Citadelle of Quebec sit high above the city under a blue sky.
A huge curtain of white water plunges down the tall cliff at Montmorency Falls just outside Quebec City.
